dailydecisions
  1. index
  2. /
  3. tools-for-thought

Tools for Thought, Minus the Manifesto

320 words·2 min read·07 may 2026·#pkm#tools#craft

Most “tools for thought” decks are manifestos in disguise. They sell a worldview and ship a backlog. The honest version is much smaller and much more boring.

Quote

A tool for thought earns the name when it survives a boring Tuesday — not when it photographs well in a launch thread.

What the loop actually is#

Capture is cheap; the value is in what the tool makes you do next:

sequenceDiagram
  participant You
  participant Tool
  participant Future You
  You->>Tool: capture, badly, fast
  Tool->>Tool: sit on it
  Future You->>Tool: search for something else
  Tool-->>Future You: surface the old note
  Future You->>Tool: prune or promote

The manifesto sells the first arrow. The work is all in the last one — pruning and promoting — which is unglamorous and never demoed.

The whole surface fits on one small map — and the manifestos almost always overbuild the left branch while starving the right:

@startmindmap
* tool for thought
** capture
*** fast
*** badly
** retrieval
*** search
*** surface old notes
** maintenance
*** prune
*** promote
@endmindmap

The test#

Does this note pass the one-sentence test?

If you cannot summarise it in a single sentence a week later, it was entertainment. That is allowed — just file it as such, and stop guilt-tripping yourself about the backlog.

A note that stuck is one I can summarise in a single sentence a week later. The rest was entertainment, which is fine, as long as I stop pretending otherwise. The discipline here rhymes with a shipping-cadence: a steady, unromantic loop beats a brilliant burst.

vault.teamanmade slowly
